---
title: "Volumes: morphology, distance, and signed-distance fields"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
A voxel grid is the discrete stand-in for a region of space, the same way a
triangle mesh is the discrete stand-in for a surface. This page shows that the
three workhorses of volumetric geometry — **morphology**, the **Euclidean
distance transform**, and the **signed-distance field** — are *not three
algorithms*. They are **one machine**: the same gather-the-neighbourhood,
then-reduce pattern you already met as mesh smoothing in
[the operators page](operators.qmd#sec-semiring), with the **algebra swapped**.

- **Dilation** is that machine under **max-plus** $(\max,+)$.
- **Erosion** is the same machine under **min-plus** $(\min,+)$.
- The **exact Euclidean distance transform** is a **min-plus matmul** — a
  single-hop shortest path against a squared-distance matrix.
- A **signed-distance field** is two distance transforms differenced.

Change the algebra, not the kernel. That is the whole page in one sentence; the
rest earns it.
:::

## A motivating question {#sec-motivation}

Here is a concrete neuroimaging task that this page answers end to end.

You have run a skull-strip on a T1-weighted MRI and the result is a **brain
mask** — a 3-D binary volume, one where there is brain, zero everywhere else.
It is not clean. The thresholding left a few stray voxels floating in the
background (bits of dura, a sliver of eyeball), it bridged the brain to the
optic nerve through a one-voxel-wide neck, and it punched small holes where a
dark vessel dipped below threshold. You need four things from it:

1. **Tidy the mask** — drop the floating specks, fill the interior holes,
   sever the thin spurious neck, and keep only the single largest blob (the
   brain).
2. **Measure how deep you are inside** — for every interior voxel, how far is
   it to the nearest boundary? (Distance maps drive sulcal-depth estimates,
   erosion-based tissue priors, and watershed seeds.)
3. **Represent the boundary as a field**, not a set of voxels, so it can be
   resampled, differentiated, and handed to a surface extractor.
4. **Hand that field to marching cubes** to get a cortical-style surface mesh.

Steps 1–3 live here. Step 4 is the bridge to
[the volume↔surface page](volume-surface.qmd#sec-marching), and the field we
build in step 3 is *exactly* the thing it consumes. We will keep returning to
this mask.

A note on coordinates before we start. Everything on this page lives in **index
space**: a voxel is addressed by its integer grid index, and "distance" is
measured in **voxels**, not millimetres. That is a deliberate simplification we
will have to pay for later — real MRI voxels are not cubes (a common spacing is
$1\times1\times3$ mm), and we flag exactly where that bites in
[the EDT section](#sec-edt).

## Mathematical morphology {#sec-morphology}

### The intuition: slide a probe over the image

Mathematical morphology is what you get when you stop thinking about a shape's
*pixels* and start thinking about a *probe* you slide across it. The probe is
called the **structuring element** (SE), written $b$ — a little stamp, often a
$3\times3$ or $3\times3\times3$ box, with one cell marked as its centre. You
place the probe's centre over each voxel in turn and ask a local question.
There are two basic questions, and they generate everything else.

**Dilation** asks: *"reaching out through the probe, what is the largest value I
can grab?"* You stamp the maximum reachable value down at the centre. On a
binary mask this **grows** the foreground: any background voxel within reach of
a foreground voxel flips on. Bumps swell, gaps close, the shape fattens.

**Erosion** asks the mirror question: *"what is the smallest value within
reach?"* It stamps the minimum down. On a binary mask this **shrinks** the
foreground: a voxel survives only if its entire probe footprint was foreground.
Thin protrusions vanish, the shape slims, and — crucially for our skull-strip —
a one-voxel neck connecting two blobs gets cut.

### From intuition to formula

Write $x$ for the image, $i$ for an output position, $p$ for an offset ranging
over the probe's footprint, and $b[p]$ for the SE's value at that offset. The
two questions become two formulas:

$$
\operatorname{dilate}(x)[i] \;=\; \max_{p}\,\bigl(x[i+p] + b[p]\bigr),
\qquad
\operatorname{erode}(x)[i] \;=\; \min_{p}\,\bigl(x[i+p] - b[p]\bigr).
$$ {#eq-dilate-erode}

Read @eq-dilate-erode slowly, because every later result is a rearrangement of
it. To produce one output voxel we (i) **gather** the neighbourhood
$\{x[i+p]\}$, (ii) **combine** each neighbour with the SE weight — *add* it for
dilation, *subtract* it for erosion — and (iii) **reduce** the combined values
with a $\max$ (dilation) or a $\min$ (erosion). Gather, combine, reduce. Hold
that shape in mind; it is the machine.

The SE values $b[p]$ let the probe be *non-flat* — a paraboloid SE, for
instance, gives grayscale morphology that respects intensity. But the
overwhelmingly common case in neuroimaging is a **flat** SE, $b\equiv 0$, where
the combine step does nothing and @eq-dilate-erode collapses to a plain sliding-
window max or min:

$$
\operatorname{dilate}_{\text{flat}}(x)[i] = \max_{p} x[i+p],
\qquad
\operatorname{erode}_{\text{flat}}(x)[i] = \min_{p} x[i+p].
$$ {#eq-flat}

This matches the `scipy.ndimage.grey_dilation` / `grey_erosion` convention
exactly, which is the contract `nitrix` chose so a port from scipy "just works".

### The two routines, and the one rule

`dilate` and `erode` (both **DIFF-JAX** — jittable and differentiable) live in
`morphology/_mm.py`. Their signatures take a single-channel array
`x: (..., *spatial)` — no explicit channel dimension; the neuroimaging-native
shape is a 3-D volume or 4-D volume-plus-time, and a multi-channel input is a
`jax.vmap` away. You choose the probe with **either** `structuring_element=` (an
explicit array, possibly non-flat) **or** `size=` (an int or per-axis tuple
giving a flat box, default `3`).

There is a genuinely important implementation fork inside these functions, and
it is worth understanding because it recurs across the library:

- **Flat box → fused `lax.reduce_window`.** When you pass only `size=`, the SE
  is flat, and @eq-flat is exactly a pooling operation. `nitrix` routes it to
  `lax.reduce_window` with `lax.max` / `lax.min`, which XLA lowers to a single
  fused kernel — no intermediate tensor. On an L4 GPU at $256^2$ this flips
  `erode`/`dilate` from $\sim\!1.3\times$ *slower* than cupy to $\sim\!1.3\times$
  *faster*, and drops peak HBM from 72 MB to 0.79 MB.
- **Explicit SE → `semiring_conv`.** When you pass an actual
  `structuring_element`, the general @eq-dilate-erode runs through
  `semiring_conv` (the next section). That path materialises the neighbourhood
  as an *im2col* patch tensor — the memory cost we forward-reference in
  [the backend page](performance.qmd#sec-backend) — but it handles arbitrary,
  non-flat probes.

A subtle correctness note that the design record calls out: the fast path is
`jit(grad(...))`-clean **only because the window's identity value is a concrete
scalar**. `lax.reduce_window` routes a generic `lax.max` reducer to its
*differentiable* specialised primitive only when JAX sees a concrete init equal
to the dtype's max identity ($-\infty$). A *traced* init — which a careless
`jnp.asarray(-inf)` silently becomes under `jit` — defeats that detection and
breaks `jit(grad)`. `nitrix` sources the identity from the same `Semiring` the
explicit path uses and passes it through `np.asarray` (which stays concrete), so
both paths agree on boundary handling *by construction* and both stay
differentiable. The take-away rule, which you will see again: **a HOST-side,
data-dependent value must not leak into a jitted, differentiable loop as a
tracer** — here it would silently disable the gradient.

One more shared behaviour: integer and boolean inputs are promoted to `float32`
at the op boundary (`_to_float`). Grayscale morphology is real-valued, the
tropical identities $\pm\infty$ are only representable in a float dtype, and
promoting once keeps the subgradient well defined and avoids a $-\infty\!\to\!$
int overflow.

### Padding and non-Euclidean boundaries

The default `padding="SAME"` pads with the **algebra identity** — $-\infty$ for
dilation's max, $+\infty$ for erosion's min — so padded positions are inert
under the reduction and the output keeps the input's spatial shape. For
non-Euclidean grids (a periodic/toroidal field, or the equirectangular sphere
grid that parameterises a cortical surface) the design *deliberately does not*
add a `padding="periodic"` mode. Instead you pre-pad with the right topology
helper and call with `padding="VALID"`:

```python
from nitrix.geometry import sphere_grid_pad_2d
se_radius = (size - 1) // 2
padded = sphere_grid_pad_2d(mask, pad=se_radius)
result = dilate(padded, size=size, padding="VALID")  # consumes exactly the pad
```

Composition is more general than a flag (it also handles the pole-flip the
sphere grid needs, which a toroidal `wrap` cannot) and costs two lines.

### Compositions: opening and closing

Erosion and dilation are *not* inverses — erode-then-dilate does not give you
back what you started with, and that asymmetry is exactly what makes them
useful. The two canonical compositions (`open` and `close`, both **DIFF-JAX**,
each a two-line chain of the primitives) are:

$$
\operatorname{open}(x) = \operatorname{dilate}(\operatorname{erode}(x)),
\qquad
\operatorname{close}(x) = \operatorname{erode}(\operatorname{dilate}(x)).
$$ {#eq-open-close}

The intuition for @eq-open-close is worth spelling out. **Opening** erodes
first: anything thinner than the probe is annihilated and *cannot be brought
back* by the following dilation, while structures that survived the erosion are
restored to roughly their original size. Net effect: **remove small bright
specks, keep the body**. **Closing** is the dual — dilate first to swell across
small dark gaps, then erode back — so it **fills small dark holes, keeps the
body**. These are precisely the two cleanups our skull-strip needs: opening
deletes the floating specks, closing fills the vessel holes.

You can build the rest of the morphological vocabulary from these by hand: the
**morphological gradient** $\operatorname{dilate}(x)-\operatorname{erode}(x)$
outlines boundaries; the **white top-hat** $x-\operatorname{open}(x)$ isolates
the bright specks the opening removed. `nitrix` does not ship these as named
symbols — they are one-line compositions — but naming the pattern makes the
public surface (`dilate`, `erode`, `open`, `close`) feel complete rather than
sparse.

@fig-volumes-morphology shows all four on a 2-D mask with our skull-strip's
pathologies built in.

```{python}
#| label: fig-volumes-morphology
#| fig-cap: "Morphology on a binary mask. Erosion severs the thin neck and deletes the floating specks; opening (dilate∘erode) removes the specks while restoring the body; closing (erode∘dilate) fills the punched hole while keeping the specks. The 3×3 flat structuring element is inset on the first panel."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import Rectangle

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# A binary shape: two blobs joined by a thin neck, a punched hole, floating specks.
N = 64
yy, xx = np.mgrid[0:N, 0:N]

def disk(cy, cx, r):
    return (yy - cy) ** 2 + (xx - cx) ** 2 <= r ** 2

shape = disk(24, 20, 11) | disk(24, 44, 11)
shape |= (np.abs(yy - 24) <= 1) & (xx >= 20) & (xx <= 44)   # 3-px neck
shape &= ~disk(24, 20, 3)                                    # punched hole
for _ in range(8):                                           # floating specks
    cy, cx = rng.integers(4, N - 4, size=2)
    if not shape[cy, cx]:
        shape |= disk(cy, cx, 1)
img = shape.astype(float)

# Flat 3x3 SE: erode = window-min (+inf pad), dilate = window-max (-inf pad).
def window_reduce(a, op, pad_val):
    p = np.pad(a, 1, mode="constant", constant_values=pad_val)
    stack = [p[1 + dy:1 + dy + N, 1 + dx:1 + dx + N]
             for dy in (-1, 0, 1) for dx in (-1, 0, 1)]
    return op(np.stack(stack, 0), axis=0)

erode  = lambda a: window_reduce(a, np.min, np.inf)
dilate = lambda a: window_reduce(a, np.max, -np.inf)
opening = lambda a: dilate(erode(a))     # remove bright specks
closing = lambda a: erode(dilate(a))     # fill dark holes

panels = [
    (img,         "original (thin neck, hole, specks)"),
    (erode(img),  "erode: neck severed, specks gone"),
    (opening(img),"open = dilate(erode): specks removed, body kept"),
    (closing(img),"close = erode(dilate): hole filled, body kept"),
]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(8.2, 8.2))
for ax, (data, title) in zip(axes.ravel(), panels):
    ax.imshow(data, cmap="Greys", origin="lower", interpolation="nearest")
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=9)
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])

ax0 = axes[0, 0]
for i in range(3):
    for j in range(3):
        ax0.add_patch(Rectangle((1 + j, 1 + i), 1, 1,
                                facecolor="tab:orange", edgecolor="k", lw=0.6))
ax0.text(2.5, 5.3, "3x3 flat SE", color="tab:orange", fontsize=8, ha="center")

fig.suptitle("Morphology on a binary mask: erode/dilate as window min/max",
             fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout(rect=(0, 0, 1, 0.97))
plt.show()
```

### The semiring connection, made literal

We have been using the words *gather*, *combine*, *reduce* on purpose. That is
the exact shape of a **semiring** contraction from
[the operators page](operators.qmd#sec-semiring): a `combine` operator $\otimes$
and a `reduce` operator $\oplus$. Mesh smoothing was the **REAL** semiring,
$(\oplus,\otimes)=(+,\times)$ — multiply neighbours by weights, sum. Morphology
is the same `semiring_conv` kernel with the algebra swapped:

| operation | algebra | identity | `combine` $\otimes$ | `reduce` $\oplus$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| blur / smooth | `REAL` | $0$ | $\times$ | $+$ |
| dilate | `TROPICAL_MAX_PLUS` | $-\infty$ | $+$ | $\max$ |
| erode | `TROPICAL_MIN_PLUS` | $+\infty$ | $+$ (with $-b$) | $\min$ |

These three algebras are the real module-level instances in
`semiring/algebras.py`: `REAL`, `TROPICAL_MAX_PLUS`, `TROPICAL_MIN_PLUS` (all
`StrictSemiring`). `dilate` calls `semiring_conv(..., semiring=TROPICAL_MAX_PLUS)`;
`erode` calls it with `TROPICAL_MIN_PLUS` *and the negated SE* `-se`, so the
algebra's native $+$ produces the conventional $-$ of @eq-dilate-erode. The
$\pm\infty$ identities are not decoration: under $\max$, adding $-\infty$ is a
no-op, so $-\infty$ is exactly the value padded positions must take to vanish
from the reduction — the same role $0$ plays for a sum.

The dividend is concrete and is the thesis of the whole module: **morphology
ships zero new kernels and zero new gradients.** The backward pass comes "for
free" because the tropical matmul already carries a hand-derived
`custom_vjp` — for $\max$-plus the subgradient is an **argmax one-hot** (each
output routes its gradient to the single neighbour that attained the max), and
for $\min$-plus an **argmin one-hot**. That is precisely what you would derive
by hand for $\partial \max / \partial x_p$, and it is why `open` and `close`
differentiate through both passes by the chain rule with no extra code.

### Mask cleanup: connected components

Opening and closing fix *local* defects, but our skull-strip still has a global
one: even after opening removes the specks, what if two genuinely separate blobs
survive and we want only the brain? That is a **connectivity** question, not a
morphology one, and it is answered by labelling.

`connected_components(mask, *, connectivity=1)` (**DIFF-JAX** in the weak sense
that it is fully jittable — its *output* is integer labels, so gradients are not
the point) returns an integer image: `0` is background, `1..K` are the connected
foreground regions, contiguously numbered. `connectivity` follows the scipy
rule: an offset is a neighbour when $\sum|\text{offset}| \le$ `connectivity`, so
`connectivity=1` is face-adjacency and `connectivity=ndim` includes every
diagonal.

The algorithm is a small gem worth understanding, because a naïve flood fill is
inherently sequential and would not jit to a fixed shape. The method is **label
propagation with pointer jumping**:

- **Seed.** Each foreground voxel takes a *unique* label — its flat index plus
  one.
- **Flood.** Each pass replaces a voxel's label with the maximum label over
  itself and its neighbours. Left alone, this is an $O(d)$ flood for a component
  of diameter $d$: the maximum seed crawls outward one step per pass.
- **Pointer jumping.** Here is the trick. A label $\ell$ literally *is* a flat
  index ($\ell-1$) pointing at the seed voxel that minted it — and that voxel is
  in the same component. So each voxel also adopts the label currently held by
  the voxel its own label points to. Because that pointed-to voxel has itself
  been flooding, the reach **doubles** every pass: convergence in $O(\log d)$
  passes instead of $O(d)$.

A `lax.while_loop` runs to the fixed point (every voxel ends at its component's
maximum seed; pointer jumping provably never merges distinct components), then a
final `cumsum`-based renumber makes the labels a contiguous $1..K$. Because the
label *image* has a fixed, data-independent shape throughout, the whole thing
jits.

`largest_connected_component(mask, *, connectivity=1)` is the canonical
skull-strip-tidy on top: label, `bincount` the labels, ignore background, and
return the boolean mask of the single biggest region (all-`False` if the input
is empty). That is step 1 of our motivating task, finished:
`largest_connected_component(close(open(brain_mask)))`.

### The rest of the module surface: rank filtering and pooling

Two more public routines round out the volumetric toolkit, and both are the same
gather-the-neighbourhood pattern with a different reducer — so they belong here,
not as an afterthought.

`median_filter(x, *, size=3, structuring_element=None, padding="SAME")`
(**DIFF-JAX**) is the **rank-order** sibling of `dilate` and `erode`. Where
dilation reduces a window with $\max$ and erosion with $\min$, the median filter
reduces it with the *middle* order statistic. That choice is what makes it the
go-to despeckler for salt-and-pepper noise: a single wild voxel is an extreme
value, so $\max$ and $\min$ would *propagate* it, but the median discards it
outright while leaving an edge un-blurred. The reducer is not a semiring
operation — a median is not associative the way $\max$ or $+$ is — so it cannot
ride the tropical kernel; instead the implementation gathers the window, sets
positions excluded by a `structuring_element` to `NaN`, and calls
`jnp.nanmedian` so boundary and masked-out positions median over only the
available subset. `padding="SAME"` pads with `NaN` for the same reason. The
honest performance note from the source: the sort inside `nanmedian` dominates
the cost (about $5\times$ behind cupy at $256^2$), and the only real lever would
be a hardcoded sorting network for the fixed small window — not yet landed.

`max_pool_with_indices_nd` and `max_unpool_nd` are the **pooling pair** that
turns the same window-max into a *resolution* operation, the volumetric analogue
of graph pooling on the [graph-geometry page](graph-geometry.qmd#sec-pooling).
`max_pool_with_indices_nd(x, *, pool_size, spatial_rank)` takes a
channel-first `(*batch, C, *spatial)` array, partitions each spatial axis into
non-overlapping windows of `pool_size` (the axis must divide evenly — there is
no internal padding, so pad upstream if needed), and returns **two** arrays: the
per-window, per-channel maxima `pooled`, and the flattened **argmax indices**
into the original single-channel spatial grid. Keeping the indices is the whole
point: `max_unpool_nd(x, indices, *, output_shape, spatial_rank)` scatters
pooled values back to the higher-resolution grid *at exactly the voxels they came
from*, zero elsewhere — the unpool that a U-Net-style encoder/decoder needs to
restore spatial detail without a learned upsampler. Both are **DIFF-JAX**;
because the max routes its gradient to the recorded argmax, pooling-then-unpooling
is a gather/scatter pair whose backward pass is the transpose of its forward, the
same one-hot routing the tropical algebra gave us above.

## The Euclidean distance transform {#sec-edt}

### The first algorithm a reader would reach for, and why it is too slow

Step 2 of our task: for every interior voxel, how far to the nearest boundary?
The **distance transform** answers exactly that. Mark every boundary voxel (in
`nitrix`'s convention, every *zero* of the mask) as a **seed** at distance $0$,
and ask each remaining voxel for its distance to the nearest seed.

The first algorithm a reader would reach for: for each voxel, loop over all
seeds and take the minimum distance. Correct, and hopelessly slow — $O(N \cdot S)$ for
$N$ voxels and $S$ seeds, both of which scale with the volume. We need the same
answer without the double loop. The route there is a beautiful little chain of
two ideas.

### The clean formula

Encode "seed or not" as a cost field $g$: $g[q]=0$ where $q$ is a seed,
$g[q]=+\infty$ otherwise. Then the **squared** distance at $p$ is

$$
d^2(p) \;=\; \min_{q}\,\bigl(g[q] + \lVert q - p\rVert^2\bigr).
$$ {#eq-edt}

In words: stand at $p$, look at every other position $q$, pay $g[q]$ (free if
$q$ is a seed, $+\infty$ if not) plus the squared distance to walk there, and
keep the cheapest. The $+\infty$ on non-seeds means only seeds ever win, so this
*is* "distance to the nearest seed" — written as a minimisation we can
restructure. We will take the square root once at the very end (working in
squared distance is what makes the next idea exact).

### Idea one: separability

Euclidean squared distance splits across axes:
$\lVert q-p\rVert^2 = \sum_a (q_a - p_a)^2$. Because the cost in @eq-edt is a
*sum* of per-axis terms and the operation outside is a $\min$, the whole ND
minimisation factors into a **sequence of 1-D passes, one per axis**, with a
single $\sqrt{}$ at the end. This is the heart of the classic exact-EDT result:
an ND distance transform is a chain of 1-D distance transforms composed along
each axis. `_distance_transform_edt` does exactly this — loop `for axis in
range(ndim)`, transform along that axis, then `sqrt`.

### Idea two: a 1-D pass *is* a min-plus matmul

Now zoom into one 1-D pass along an axis of length $n$:

$$
\operatorname{out}[p] \;=\; \min_{q}\,\bigl(g[q] + (q-p)^2\bigr).
$$ {#eq-edt-1d}

Stare at @eq-edt-1d next to the semiring contraction
$C[i,j]=\bigoplus_k A[i,k]\otimes B[k,j]$. With $\oplus=\min$ and $\otimes=+$
(the **min-plus** algebra), it is *identical* to a matrix–vector product
$g \otimes D^2$ where $D^2[q,p]=(q-p)^2$ is the fixed **squared-distance
matrix**. Reshape the off-axis dimensions into a batch of independent lines, and
the entire axis pass is one call:

```python
out = semiring_matmul(g_lines, D2, semiring=TROPICAL_MIN_PLUS)  # (lines, n) @ (n, n)
```

This is the page's deepest point. The EDT is not "like" a shortest path — under
min-plus, a matmul *is* a single-hop shortest path: from each $p$, the cheapest
single edge to any $q$, where the edge weight is $g[q]+(q-p)^2$. Distance
transform, shortest path, and matrix multiplication are the same computation
seen in three vocabularies, and `nitrix` exploits that to reuse the tuned
`semiring_matmul` kernel verbatim.

`_edt_along_axis` does precisely this — `moveaxis` the target axis to the end,
build `d2 = (pos[:, None] - pos[None, :]) ** 2`, call `semiring_matmul` under
`TROPICAL_MIN_PLUS`. One implementation subtlety: it seeds non-boundary
positions with a large **finite** sentinel `_EDT_BIG = 1e10` rather than literal
`+inf`, so the min-plus accumulation never propagates `inf`; positions still
above the threshold after all axes (no boundary ever reachable) are mapped to
`+inf` at the very end. Working in `float32` with a finite sentinel keeps
`BIG + d^2` exact.

### The public surface

Two **DIFF-JAX** entry points expose this:

- `distance_transform(mask, *, metric="euclidean", structuring_element=None,
  max_iters=None, backend="auto")`. With the default `metric="euclidean"` and no
  explicit SE, you get the **exact** EDT via the separable min-plus-matmul
  engine above. This is the metric scipy / cupy / ITK all mean by "distance
  transform", and the result matches `scipy.ndimage.distance_transform_edt` to
  floating-point round-off. The return is a distance map of the same shape;
  `+inf` where the mask is everywhere non-zero (no boundary reachable).
- `distance_transform_edt(mask, *, backend="auto")` — a thin scipy-named alias
  for the Euclidean path.

### The design story: why they dropped Felzenszwalb–Huttenlocher

This is one of the most instructive decisions in the codebase, and it is the
"rigour meets practice" moment the unit keeps promising. The textbook exact-EDT
algorithm is **Felzenszwalb–Huttenlocher** (F–H): each 1-D pass computes the
**lower envelope of a family of parabolas** (one upward parabola per seed,
$(q-p)^2 + g[q]$), in $O(n)$ work per line — *lower* asymptotic work than the
matmul's $O(n^2)$.

`nitrix` implemented F–H first. It is correct and exact. Then they **dropped
it**, and the reason is the central tension of GPU numerics: F–H's lower
envelope is a **data-dependent stack** — push and pop at a dynamically computed
index. Rendered in pure JAX (nested `lax.while_loop` plus a dynamic-index
scatter under `vmap`) it lowers to a swarm of tiny control-flow-bound kernels:
$\sim\!18$ ms at $64^3$ on the L4, about **80× behind cupy**.

The min-plus matmul does *more* arithmetic ($O(n^2)$ per axis) but it is
**dense, branch-free, and rides the already-tuned semiring streaming kernel** —
so the GPU devours it: $0.24$ ms at $64^3$ (parity with cupy), and it *beats*
scipy on CPU. There is no realistic size where F–H's lower work wins back its
control-flow constant. The lesson, in miniature, is the morphology thesis again:
the right move was **not** a bespoke EDT kernel, but expressing the EDT in the
*existing* algebra, even at a higher FLOP count. On a GPU, dense-and-regular
beats clever-and-branchy almost every time.

### The chamfer fallback, and a real gap

For non-Euclidean metrics, `distance_transform` keeps an **iterative chamfer**
engine, selected by `metric="chebyshev"` (chessboard), `metric="city_block"`
(Manhattan), or a custom `structuring_element` (which selects the chamfer path
regardless of `metric`). Each iteration is one `TROPICAL_MIN_PLUS`
`semiring_conv` with a step-cost SE that adds the per-step distance to each
neighbour and takes the running min. Iterate `max_iters` times (default: the
longest spatial extent, enough for exact convergence). This is **Bellman–Ford
relaxation** — propagate the shortest-path estimate one hop per pass until it
stops changing — and it is the same min-plus algebra as the EDT, applied
*iteratively* through a small kernel rather than *once* through a dense matrix.
It is bit-exact with `scipy.ndimage.distance_transform_cdt`.

::: {.callout-warning title="Anisotropic spacing: a known, unlanded gap"}
The Euclidean engine **bakes unit voxel spacing into the squared-distance
matrix** $D^2[q,p]=(q-p)^2$. There is no `sampling=` parameter, so it cannot
compute the EDT on an anisotropic grid — e.g. a $1\times1\times3$ mm MRI, the
common clinical case. scipy's `distance_transform_edt(input, sampling=(...))`
and ITK both support this; `nitrix` silently returns the *unit-spacing* answer,
which is **wrong** on anisotropic data (off by more than a voxel on a
$1\times1\times3$ grid). The fix is cheap and known — scale each axis's matrix
to $D^2[q,p]=(s_a(q-p))^2$ — but as of this writing it has **not landed**. Until
it does, rescale your understanding accordingly, or use the chamfer engine's
custom-SE escape hatch. (Feature request: `distance-transform-anisotropic-sampling`.)
:::

@fig-volumes-edt validates the engine the way the unit insists — against an
**analytic oracle**, not a golden file. Inside a disk of radius $R$, the exact
distance to the boundary is $R - r$ where $r$ is the distance from the centre.
The computed EDT tracks that line to within a voxel (the residual is pure grid
discretisation, and it shrinks under refinement — the convergence-under-
refinement theme made concrete).

```{python}
#| label: fig-volumes-edt
#| fig-cap: "Exact Euclidean distance transform of a binary disk via the separable min-plus matmul. Left: the EDT as a height field — a cone peaking along the medial axis (here the centre). Middle: distance contours. Right: the radial profile against the analytic oracle R − r; agreement is to sub-voxel, the residual being grid discretisation."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A binary disk: 1 inside (interior), 0 outside (the boundary seeds).
N, c, R = 81, 40, 24.0
yy, xx = np.mgrid[0:N, 0:N]
mask = ((yy - c) ** 2 + (xx - c) ** 2 <= R ** 2).astype(float)

BIG = 1e10
g = np.where(mask == 0, 0.0, BIG)        # seeds cost 0, interior costs BIG

def edt_axis(g, axis):
    # one 1-D squared-EDT pass = a min-plus matmul against D2[q,p] = (q-p)^2
    g = np.moveaxis(g, axis, -1)
    n = g.shape[-1]
    pos = np.arange(n)
    D2 = (pos[:, None] - pos[None, :]) ** 2
    lines = g.reshape(-1, n)
    out = np.min(lines[:, :, None] + D2[None, :, :], axis=1)  # min over q
    return np.moveaxis(out.reshape(g.shape), -1, axis)

for ax in range(2):
    g = edt_axis(g, ax)
edt = np.sqrt(g)

rad = np.sqrt((yy - c) ** 2 + (xx - c) ** 2)
analytic = np.where(mask > 0, np.maximum(R - rad, 0.0), 0.0)

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(11, 4.2))

ax1 = fig.add_subplot(1, 3, 1, projection="3d")
X, Y = np.meshgrid(np.arange(N), np.arange(N))
ax1.plot_surface(X, Y, edt, cmap="viridis", linewidth=0, antialiased=True)
ax1.set_title("EDT(mask) as a height field\n(cone peaks at the medial axis)", fontsize=9)
ax1.set_xticks([]); ax1.set_yticks([]); ax1.set_zticks([])

ax2 = fig.add_subplot(1, 3, 2)
im = ax2.imshow(edt, cmap="viridis", origin="lower")
ax2.contour(edt, levels=np.arange(2, 24, 4), colors="white", linewidths=0.6)
ax2.set_title("EDT contours (distance rings)", fontsize=9)
ax2.set_xticks([]); ax2.set_yticks([])
fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax2, fraction=0.046, pad=0.04)

ax3 = fig.add_subplot(1, 3, 3)
order = np.argsort(rad[mask > 0])
r_in = rad[mask > 0][order]
ax3.plot(r_in, edt[mask > 0][order], ".", ms=2, label="EDT (min-plus matmul)")
ax3.plot(r_in, analytic[mask > 0][order], "-", lw=1.5, color="k", label="analytic  R - r")
ax3.set_xlabel("radius r from centre"); ax3.set_ylabel("distance to boundary")
ax3.set_title("vs analytic oracle (R = 24)", fontsize=9)
ax3.legend(fontsize=8)
err = np.max(np.abs(edt[mask > 0] - analytic[mask > 0]))
ax3.text(0.5, 0.92, f"max|err| = {err:.3f} voxel", transform=ax3.transAxes,
         fontsize=8, ha="center")

fig.suptitle("Exact Euclidean distance transform: separable min-plus matmul vs analytic disk",
             fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout(rect=(0, 0, 1, 0.95))
plt.show()
```

## Signed-distance fields {#sec-sdf}

### The intuition: a shape as a field, not a set

So far a shape has been a *set* of voxels — on or off. Step 3 of our task wants
something richer: a **field** defined everywhere, that is **negative inside** the
shape, **zero on the boundary**, and **positive outside**, with magnitude equal
to the distance to the boundary. This is the **signed-distance field** (SDF),
written $s(x)$. (We use $s$, not $\phi$ — $\phi$ is reserved for the eigen-
functions of [the spectral page](spectral.qmd#sec-eigen).)

Why bother turning a crisp mask into a smooth field? Three reasons that matter
downstream. A field can be **resampled** to any resolution without the staircase
artefacts a binary mask suffers. A field is **differentiable** — its zero level
set moves smoothly as the field changes, which is exactly what learned level-set
surface models need. And the field's **zero crossing is a sub-voxel boundary**:
where the mask only knew "this voxel is inside, that one is outside", the SDF
interpolates the true surface to between voxels — the property the next chapter's
marching cubes exploits.

### The composition (there is no single symbol)

The SDF of a mask is built — by you, in two lines — from the distance transform.
The unsigned distance from the boundary is `distance_transform(mask)` *inside*
and `distance_transform(~mask)` *outside*; sign one negative and subtract:

$$
s \;=\; \operatorname{EDT}(\lnot\text{mask}) \;-\; \operatorname{EDT}(\text{mask}).
$$ {#eq-sdf}

There is **no `mask_to_sdf` symbol** in `nitrix`; the SDF-from-mask route *is*
this composition, and that is deliberate — it stays pure-JAX and differentiable
because both terms are the **DIFF-JAX** `distance_transform`. Read @eq-sdf
against the convention: an *interior* voxel has $\operatorname{EDT}(\text{mask})>0$
(it is far from a background seed) and $\operatorname{EDT}(\lnot\text{mask})=0$
(it is itself foreground), so $s<0$ — **negative inside**, as required. Flip the
two terms and you would get the opposite sign convention; both appear in the
literature, so always check which one a downstream consumer wants. Marching cubes
in `nitrix` treats a corner as "inside" when `volume < level` with `level=0`, so
**negative-inside** is the convention to feed it.

A precision footnote: away from the boundary, $\operatorname{EDT}(\text{mask})$
and $\operatorname{EDT}(\lnot\text{mask})$ are each off by up to half a voxel
because the seeds sit *on* grid voxels, not on the true continuous boundary
between them. The SDF inherits that half-voxel offset; it is the discrete-
approximates-smooth tax, and it shrinks under refinement.

@fig-volumes-sdf validates the composition against the analytic SDF of a circle,
$s = r - R$, and shows the recovered zero contour landing on the true circle.

```{python}
#| label: fig-volumes-sdf
#| fig-cap: "Signed-distance field of a disk, s = EDT(¬mask) − EDT(mask): negative (blue) inside, positive (red) outside. Left: the recovered zero level set (black) sits on the true circle (green dashed). Right: the radial profile against the analytic circle SDF r − R."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

N, c, R = 81, 40, 24.0
yy, xx = np.mgrid[0:N, 0:N]
rad = np.sqrt((yy - c) ** 2 + (xx - c) ** 2)
mask = (rad <= R).astype(float)

def edt(mask):
    BIG = 1e10
    g = np.where(mask == 0, 0.0, BIG)
    for axis in range(mask.ndim):
        g = np.moveaxis(g, axis, -1)
        n = g.shape[-1]
        pos = np.arange(n)
        D2 = (pos[:, None] - pos[None, :]) ** 2
        lines = g.reshape(-1, n)
        out = np.min(lines[:, :, None] + D2[None, :, :], axis=1).reshape(g.shape)
        g = np.moveaxis(out, -1, axis)
    return np.sqrt(g)

# negative inside: outside-distance minus inside-distance
sdf = edt(1.0 - mask) - edt(mask)
analytic = rad - R                       # true SDF of a circle of radius R

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4.4))

ax = axes[0]
vmax = np.abs(sdf).max()
im = ax.imshow(sdf, cmap="RdBu_r", origin="lower", vmin=-vmax, vmax=vmax)
ax.contour(sdf, levels=[0], colors="k", linewidths=2.0)
theta = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 200)
ax.plot(c + R * np.cos(theta), c + R * np.sin(theta), "--",
        color="lime", lw=1.2, label="true circle r = R")
ax.set_title("s = EDT(~mask) - EDT(mask)\nblack = recovered zero set", fontsize=9)
ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([]); ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="lower right")
fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax, fraction=0.046, pad=0.04)

ax = axes[1]
order = np.argsort(rad.ravel())
ax.plot(rad.ravel()[order], sdf.ravel()[order], ".", ms=1.5, label="composed SDF")
ax.plot(rad.ravel()[order], analytic.ravel()[order], "-", color="k", lw=1.2,
        label="analytic  r - R")
ax.axhline(0, color="gray", lw=0.6); ax.axvline(R, color="gray", lw=0.6, ls=":")
ax.set_xlabel("radius r from centre"); ax.set_ylabel("signed distance s")
ax.set_title("radial profile vs analytic r - R", fontsize=9); ax.legend(fontsize=8)

fig.suptitle("Signed-distance field from a mask (negative inside) vs analytic circle",
             fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout(rect=(0, 0, 1, 0.94))
plt.show()
```

### The bridge to surfaces

The SDF is where this page hands off to the next. Its **zero level set** is a
surface — a closed curve in 2-D, a closed surface in 3-D — and extracting that
surface as a triangle mesh is exactly what **marching cubes** does, the opening
act of [the volume↔surface page](volume-surface.qmd#sec-marching). Feed the SDF
we composed above to `geometry.marching_cubes(volume, level=0.0)` (a **HOST-CTOR**
op: host-side NumPy, data-dependent vertex count, not differentiable) and you
get the cortical-style mesh of step 4.

There is a second SDF route worth knowing about, and an asymmetry between them.
The route on this page goes **mask → field**, by differencing two EDTs. The
route in the next chapter, `geometry.mesh_to_sdf(mesh, shape, *, spacing=...)`,
goes the other way — **mesh → field** — rasterising a triangle mesh to a signed-
distance volume (exact point-to-triangle distance, signed by the generalised
winding number). Note the asymmetry the brief flags: `mesh_to_sdf` **takes a
`spacing=`** argument and so handles anisotropic grids, whereas the EDT route
(this page) **does not yet** — the same gap from the warning above. The two
routes together let you round-trip mask → field → mesh → field and check the
results agree, which is precisely the field↔mesh validation the next chapter
builds on.

## One machine, many algebras {#sec-one-machine}

Step back and look at what we built. Every operation on this page was the same
**gather the neighbourhood, then reduce** — the thesis from
[the operators page](operators.qmd#sec-semiring) — with only the algebra
changing:

| operation | $\oplus$ (reduce) | $\otimes$ (combine) | identity | what it does |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| blur / smooth | $+$ | $\times$ | $0$ | diffuses values (REAL) |
| dilate | $\max$ | $+$ | $-\infty$ | grows / fills (max-plus) |
| erode | $\min$ | $+$ | $+\infty$ | shrinks / cuts (min-plus) |
| EDT (one axis) | $\min$ | $+$ | $+\infty$ | shortest path = matmul |
| chamfer DT | $\min$ | $+$ | $+\infty$ | iterated shortest path |

Diffusion, dilation, erosion, and distance are not four algorithms with four
gradients to maintain. They are **one** gather-and-reduce with the kernel swapped
out, and the gradient follows the algebra automatically (sum-of-products
transpose for REAL; argmax / argmin one-hot for the tropical pair). That is why
`nitrix` could ship the entire morphology-and-distance surface as $\sim\!250$
lines of glue over the semiring substrate, with zero new kernels and zero new
`custom_vjp` rules.

A tiny worked example makes the swap visceral. Take a 1-D signal and a flat
3-tap window; gather the same patches; reduce three ways. Under REAL you get a
blur, under max-plus a dilation, under min-plus an erosion — three outputs, one
op (@fig-volumes-onemachine).

```{python}
#| label: fig-volumes-onemachine
#| fig-cap: "One gather+reduce, three algebras. The same 3-tap patches of a 1-D signal, reduced under REAL (+,×) → blur, under max-plus → dilation, under min-plus → erosion. The kernel is swapped; the gather is identical."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

N = 40
x = np.zeros(N)
x[8:12] = 1.0      # a bright bar
x[20] = 1.0        # a lone speck
x[28:34] = 1.0     # a wider bar...
x[30] = 0.0        # ...with a one-sample dip

# im2col: stack the 3 shifted views (the receptive fields), SAME padding.
def patches(a, identity):
    p = np.concatenate([[identity], a, [identity]])
    return np.stack([p[0:N], p[1:N + 1], p[2:N + 2]], axis=1)   # (N, 3)

blur   = (patches(x, 0.0) * (1.0 / 3.0)).sum(axis=1)            # REAL: +, x
dilate = (patches(x, -np.inf) + 0.0).max(axis=1)               # max-plus
erode  = (patches(x,  np.inf) - 0.0).min(axis=1)               # min-plus

fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 1, figsize=(8, 6.4), sharex=True)
idx = np.arange(N)
axes[0].step(idx, x,      where="mid", color="k");          axes[0].set_ylabel("input")
axes[1].step(idx, blur,   where="mid", color="tab:blue");   axes[1].set_ylabel("REAL\n(+, x): blur")
axes[2].step(idx, dilate, where="mid", color="tab:red");    axes[2].set_ylabel("max-plus\ndilate")
axes[3].step(idx, erode,  where="mid", color="tab:green");  axes[3].set_ylabel("min-plus\nerode")
for ax in axes:
    ax.set_ylim(-0.15, 1.25); ax.grid(alpha=0.25)
axes[-1].set_xlabel("position i")
fig.suptitle("One gather+reduce, three algebras: the same patches, the kernel swapped",
             fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout(rect=(0, 0, 1, 0.96))
plt.show()
```

## Further reading

- Serra, J. (1982). *Image Analysis and Mathematical Morphology.* Academic
  Press. — The foundational text; sets and structuring elements, openings and
  closings.
- Soille, P. (2003). *Morphological Image Analysis: Principles and
  Applications* (2nd ed.). Springer. — The practitioner's reference; grayscale
  morphology, geodesic operators, watersheds.
- Felzenszwalb, P. F., & Huttenlocher, D. P. (2012). *Distance Transforms of
  Sampled Functions.* Theory of Computing, 8(19), 415–428. — The $O(n)$ lower-
  envelope-of-parabolas EDT that `nitrix` implemented and then dropped on the
  GPU.
- Maurer, C. R., Qi, R., & Raghavan, V. (2003). *A Linear Time Algorithm for
  Computing Exact Euclidean Distance Transforms of Binary Images in Arbitrary
  Dimensions.* IEEE TPAMI, 25(2), 265–270. — The exact ND EDT and the
  separability argument.
- Maclagan, D., & Sturmfels, B. (2015). *Introduction to Tropical Geometry.*
  AMS. — The mathematics of the min-plus / max-plus (tropical) semirings that
  underpin morphology and the distance transform.
- Rosenfeld, A., & Pfaltz, J. L. (1966). *Sequential Operations in Digital
  Picture Processing.* JACM, 13(4), 471–494. — The original chamfer / distance-
  propagation idea behind the iterative engine.

## See also

- `morphology/_mm.py` —
  [dilate / erode / open / close, distance_transform, EDT](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/morphology/_mm.py)
- `morphology/_label.py` —
  [connected_components / largest_connected_component](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/morphology/_label.py)
- `morphology/_median.py` —
  [median_filter (NaN-safe rank filter)](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/morphology/_median.py)
- `morphology/pooling.py` —
  [max_pool_with_indices_nd / max_unpool_nd](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/morphology/pooling.py)
- `morphology/__init__.py` —
  [the module surface](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/morphology/__init__.py)
- `semiring/algebras.py` —
  [REAL / TROPICAL_MAX_PLUS / TROPICAL_MIN_PLUS and the rest](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/algebras.py)
- `semiring/conv.py` —
  [semiring_conv (the morphology substrate)](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/conv.py)
- `semiring/matmul.py` —
  [semiring_matmul (the EDT substrate)](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/matmul.py)
- Neighbouring pages: [operators](operators.qmd#sec-semiring) ·
  [Laplace–Beltrami smoothing](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-smoothing) ·
  [volume↔surface](volume-surface.qmd#sec-marching) ·
  [performance / backends](performance.qmd#sec-backend)
